section.title: Ezra Sent to Jerusalem
section.sectionRationale: About sixty years after the temple is finished, the book's namesake finally appears. Ezra is introduced by his priestly line back to Aaron and by his heart, set to study, obey, and teach the Law. Artaxerxes sends him with a lavish letter of authority and provision, and Ezra answers with a doxology blessing the God whose good hand moved the king. It is one movement: the coming of the teacher of the Law, sent by God through the king.
section.thesis: God raises up Ezra, a priest and scribe whose heart is set to study, practice, and teach the Law, and moves a Persian king to send and fund him, so that the restoration of God's people advances not only by a rebuilt temple but by a recovered devotion to God's word.
section.frameNote: Three beats: Ezra introduced by lineage and by his resolve to know and teach the Law, verses 1 to 10; the letter of Artaxerxes granting authority and provision, verses 11 to 26; and Ezra's doxology blessing God for moving the king, verses 27 and 28.

units.label: Ezra the priest and scribe
units.note: he set his heart to study, practice, and teach

verses.text: Many years later, during the reign of Artaxerxes king of Persia, Ezra son of Seraiah, the son of Azariah, the son of Hilkiah,
the son of Shallum, the son of Zadok, the son of Ahitub,
the son of Amariah, the son of Azariah, the son of Meraioth,
the son of Zerahiah, the son of Uzzi, the son of Bukki,
the son of Abishua, the son of Phinehas, the son of Eleazar, the son of Aaron the chief priest,

this Ezra came up from Babylon. He was a {{sopher|scribe}} skilled in the Law of Moses, which the LORD, the God of Israel, had given. The king had granted Ezra all his requests, for the {{yad|hand}} of the LORD his God was upon him.
So in the seventh year of King Artaxerxes, he went up to Jerusalem with some of the Israelites, including priests, Levites, singers, gatekeepers, and temple servants.
Ezra arrived in Jerusalem in the fifth month of the seventh year of the king.
He had begun the journey from Babylon on the first day of the first month, and he arrived in Jerusalem on the first day of the fifth month, for the gracious hand of his God was upon him.
For Ezra had set his heart to {{darash|study}} the Law of the LORD, to practice it, and to teach its statutes and ordinances in Israel.

units.label: The letter of Artaxerxes
units.note: authority, provision, and freedom to teach

verses.text: This is the text of the letter King Artaxerxes had given to Ezra the priest and scribe, an expert in the commandments and statutes of the LORD to Israel:
Artaxerxes, king of kings. To Ezra the priest, the scribe of the Law of the God of heaven: Greetings.
I hereby decree that any volunteers among the Israelites in my kingdom, including the priests and Levites, may go up with you to Jerusalem.
You are sent by the king and his seven counselors to evaluate Judah and Jerusalem according to the {{dat|Law}} of your God, which is in your hand.
Moreover, you are to take with you the silver and gold that the king and his counselors have freely offered to the God of Israel, whose dwelling is in Jerusalem,
together with all the silver and gold you may find in all the province of Babylon, as well as the freewill offerings of the people and priests to the house of their God in Jerusalem.

With this money, therefore, you are to buy as many bulls, rams, and lambs as needed, together with their grain offerings and drink offerings, and offer them on the altar at the house of your God in Jerusalem.
You and your brothers may do whatever seems best with the rest of the silver and gold, according to the will of your God.
You must deliver to the God of Jerusalem all the articles given to you for the service of the house of your God.
And if anything else is needed for the house of your God that you may have occasion to supply, you may pay for it from the royal treasury.
I, King Artaxerxes, decree to all the treasurers west of the Euphrates: Whatever Ezra the priest, the scribe of the Law of the God of heaven, may require of you, it must be provided promptly,
up to a hundred talents of silver, a hundred cors of wheat, a hundred baths of wine, a hundred baths of olive oil, and salt without limit.
Whatever is commanded by the God of heaven must be done diligently for His house. For why should wrath fall on the realm of the king and his sons?
And be advised that you have no authority to impose tribute, duty, or toll on any of the priests, Levites, singers, doorkeepers, temple servants, or other servants of this house of God.

And you, Ezra, according to the wisdom of your God, which you possess, are to appoint magistrates and judges to judge all the people west of the Euphrates, all who know the laws of your God. And you are to teach these laws to anyone who does not know them.
If anyone does not keep the law of your God and the law of the king, let a strict judgment be executed against him, whether death, banishment, confiscation of property, or imprisonment.

units.label: Ezra blesses God
units.note: the hand of the LORD my God was upon me

verses.text: Blessed be the LORD, the God of our fathers, who has put into the heart of the king to so honor the house of the LORD in Jerusalem,

and who has shown me favor before the king, his counselors, and all his powerful officials. And because the hand of the LORD my God was upon me, I took courage and gathered the leaders of Israel to return with me.

sopher.gloss: A scribe, one learned in and devoted to the Scriptures. Ezra is the model sopher, skilled in the Law of Moses, whose scholarship serves obedience and teaching. He embodies the shift, in the restored community, toward a people gathered around the written word of God.

yad.gloss: Hand. The refrain the hand of the LORD his God was upon him runs through chapters 7 and 8, meaning God's gracious, empowering, and protecting favor. Every success of Ezra's mission is traced not to his skill or the king's goodwill but to God's good hand.

darash.gloss: To seek, inquire, or study diligently. Ezra set his heart to darash the Law, study aimed not at mere knowledge but at doing and teaching. The word captures a wholehearted pursuit of God through His word, the pattern the whole chapter commends.

dat.gloss: Law, decree, or edict; a Persian loanword in the Aramaic section. Strikingly, the same word names both the king's decree and the Law of God, so that in Ezra's commission God's law is carried into Judah clothed with royal authority, all under the higher rule of God.

basic.context: Sixty years after the temple was finished, the man the book is named for arrives. Ezra is a priest descended from Aaron and a scribe expert in the Law of Moses. The heart of the chapter is verse 10: Ezra set his heart to study God's Law, to practice it, and to teach it. King Artaxerxes sends him to Jerusalem with a remarkable letter granting money, authority, freedom from taxes for temple workers, and permission to appoint judges and teach the Law. Ezra responds by blessing God, who put this into the king's heart. The question is: what does the restored community most need? A people shaped by God's word.
basic.hermeneutics: This is history that holds up Ezra as a model, especially in verse 10. It is not commanding us to seek royal charters, but it is showing us what faithful ministry looks like: knowing, obeying, and teaching God's word in that order. The lavish royal grant is God's providence for a unique moment, while the enduring lesson is Ezra's devotion to the Scriptures and his humility in crediting God's hand.
basic.originalLanguages: Ezra is a sopher, a scribe, skilled in the Law, and the reason for his success is stated again and again: the yad, the hand, of the LORD was upon him. He had set his heart to darash, to study, the Law, with obedience and teaching in view. Even the king's letter calls God's Law a dat, a decree, the same word used for royal law, now carrying imperial weight.
basic.typology: Ezra the priest-scribe who studies, obeys, and teaches the word points forward to Christ, the greater teacher and priest, who not only knows and keeps God's word perfectly but is Himself the Word made flesh.
basic.christ: Ezra devoted his whole heart to the Scriptures and taught them to God's people, and in doing so he pictures Jesus, the true teacher who opens the Scriptures and shows how they all point to Him. Where Ezra studied the Law, Christ fulfills it; where Ezra taught statutes, Christ writes God's law on our hearts. The good hand of God that carried Ezra is the same favor that reaches us fully in Christ.
basic.god: God is the mover behind the whole mission. He gives Ezra a heart for His word, He puts generosity and authority into a pagan king's heart, and He lays His good hand on His servant. Ezra sees this clearly and blesses God rather than himself.
basic.crossReferences: Nehemiah 8 shows Ezra reading and explaining the Law to the people. Luke 24:27 shows Christ opening all the Scriptures about Himself. And Proverbs 21:1 explains how God turned the king's heart.
basic.oneStory: The restoration needed more than stones; it needed a people formed by God's word. God provides that by sending Ezra, whose heart is set on Scripture, and moving a king to send him. It points to Christ, the true teacher and Word, who gathers and forms the people of God by the truth.

context.literary: The chapter opens the second half of the book with a genealogy anchoring Ezra to Aaron, then states his character in the pivotal verse 10, then quotes Artaxerxes' Aramaic letter in full, and closes with Ezra's first-person doxology. The shift into Aramaic for the royal letter, verses 12 to 26, and back into Hebrew for Ezra's blessing, mirrors the movement from imperial authorization to covenant gratitude.
context.historical: Artaxerxes I sent Ezra in his seventh year, about 458 BC. Persian kings did commission trusted subjects to organize the religious and legal life of their peoples, and supporting local temples, even asking for prayers, was standard policy meant to secure divine favor and loyalty. The authority to appoint judges and teach the law over the province west of the Euphrates was a genuine and substantial grant.
context.canonical: Ezra's arrival marks the recovery of Scripture at the center of Israel's life, setting up the great reading of the Law in Nehemiah 8. His pattern of study, obedience, and teaching becomes a lasting model for the ministry of the word. In the sweep of redemptive history, the teacher who forms the people by Scripture anticipates Christ, the definitive teacher, and the church's ministry of the word that He establishes.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To introduce Ezra as a priest and scribe wholly devoted to studying, obeying, and teaching God's Law, and to show that God moved the king to send and fund him, so that readers value the ministry of the word and trace every success to God's good hand.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a unique royal commission, including civil authority to enforce law, which is not a template for church-state relations today. What is held up for imitation is Ezra's threefold devotion in verse 10 and his humble crediting of God, not the imperial machinery that surrounded his mission.

debates.question: Why does the order in verse 10, study, then practice, then teach, matter?

views.label: A deliberate, essential sequence
views.case: Ezra first studies, then does, then teaches; the order guards against hypocrisy, for one must know and obey God's word before teaching it with integrity, making verse 10 a lasting charter for faithful ministry.

views.label: A portrait of wholehearted devotion
views.case: The three verbs together depict a heart wholly set on God's word in every direction, knowing, living, and passing it on; the emphasis falls less on strict sequence than on the completeness of Ezra's commitment.

debates.bearing: Both readings commend the same ideal: teachers of Scripture must first be students and doers of it, so that their teaching flows from a life shaped by the word.

debates.question: Is the royal grant of legal authority in verses 25 and 26 a model for enforcing God's law by the state?

views.label: A unique providential provision
views.case: The authority is a specific Persian arrangement for a semi-autonomous province, given to advance the restoration; it is descriptive of God's providence in that moment, not a charter for religious coercion in every age.

views.label: A legitimate ordering under Persian law
views.case: Within its own setting, the grant rightly ordered the community's civil and religious life under recognized imperial law; it shows God using the structures of the day, without implying the church should wield the sword.

debates.bearing: Either way, the abiding lesson is the priority of teaching God's word, verse 25, while the coercive apparatus belongs to that historical moment rather than to the mission of Christ's church.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's vocabulary defines Ezra and his mission. He is a sopher, a scribe, but not a mere copyist; he is skilled in and devoted to the Law, embodying the restored community's turn toward the written word of God. The engine of his success is named repeatedly: the yad, the hand, of the LORD was upon him, a refrain that will echo through chapter 8, crediting God rather than Ezra or the king for every open door. Verse 10 sums up his heart with darash, to seek and study, a wholehearted pursuit of the Law aimed at obedience and teaching, not scholarship for its own sake. And when the king's Aramaic letter calls God's Law a dat, the ordinary word for a royal decree, it quietly shows God's word entering Judah under imperial authority, even as it rests, in truth, on an authority far higher than the throne of Persia.

typology.type: Ezra the priest-scribe devoted to God's word
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the great teacher and true priest, who opens the Scriptures and is Himself the Word made flesh, Luke 24:27 and John 1:14.

typology.type: The Law carried in Ezra's hand to God's people
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who fulfills the Law and writes it on the hearts of His people by the Spirit, Matthew 5:17 and Hebrews 8:10.

typology.type: The good hand of God sending and keeping His servant
typology.fulfillment: The favor and mission of God that rests fully on Christ and, through Him, on all He sends, John 20:21.

study.christ: Ezra gave his whole heart to God's word, studying it, obeying it, and teaching it, and in that devotion he stands as a signpost to Jesus. Christ is the greater scribe and priest, the one who not only knows and keeps the Law flawlessly but is the very Word of God in the flesh. On the Emmaus road He did what Ezra longed to do, opening all the Scriptures to show that they speak of Him. And where Ezra could only teach statutes and ordinances from the outside, Christ, by His Spirit, writes God's law on the hearts of His people, so that the ministry of the word Ezra began finds its fulfillment in the Teacher who saves those He teaches.
study.god: God is the unseen mover of the whole account. He gives Ezra a heart set on His word, He inclines the heart of a mighty king to send, fund, and empower the mission, and He lays His good hand on His servant to protect and prosper him. Ezra sees all of this and does the fitting thing: he blesses the LORD, refusing to credit his own skill or the king's favor. The God of this chapter is sovereign over kings, generous to His people, and worthy of all the glory for every good thing accomplished through His servants.

crossReferences.note: Ezra reads and explains the Law to the assembled people, the fruit of the devotion described in Ezra 7:10.
Christ, the true teacher, opens all the Scriptures concerning Himself, fulfilling the ministry of the word Ezra modeled.
The LORD turns the king's heart wherever He wills, as He turned Artaxerxes to honor God's house.
The blessing of the one who delights in and meditates on God's law, embodied in Ezra.

study.oneStory: The restored community needed more than a temple; it needed to be reformed by God's word. So God raised up Ezra, a scribe whose heart was set to study, obey, and teach the Law, and moved a king to send him with everything required. The teacher who forms God's people by Scripture points forward to Christ, the true and final teacher and the living Word, who gathers and shapes the people of God by the truth and writes His law on their hearts.

quiz.q: How does Ezra 7:10 describe Ezra's devotion?

options.text: He set his heart to study the Law, to practice it, and to teach it in Israel.
options.note: Verse 10 gives the threefold pattern of study, obedience, and teaching.

options.text: He set his heart to gain wealth and status at court.
options.note: Ezra sought God's word, not personal advancement.

options.text: He set his heart only to study, keeping it to himself.
options.note: He studied in order to obey and to teach others, not to hoard knowledge.

options.text: He set his heart to rebuild the temple walls.
options.note: The temple was already built; Ezra's focus was the Law.

quiz.q: What phrase repeatedly explains Ezra's success?

options.text: The hand of the LORD his God was upon him.
options.note: The refrain in verses 6, 9, and 28 credits God's good hand.

options.text: Ezra was cleverer than the king's counselors.
options.note: The text credits God's hand, not Ezra's cleverness.

options.text: Ezra had a large army at his command.
options.note: He had no army; his security was God's hand upon him.

options.text: The king simply liked him personally.
options.note: Even the king's favor is traced to God moving his heart.

quiz.q: What did Artaxerxes' letter authorize Ezra to do?

options.text: To take provisions and offerings, appoint judges, and teach the Law of God.
options.note: Verses 13-26 grant funding, authority, and freedom to teach and appoint judges.

options.text: To crown himself king over Judah.
options.note: No kingship is granted; Ezra is sent to teach and order the community under Persian authority.

options.text: To wage war on the surrounding nations.
options.note: The commission concerned teaching and administering God's law, not warfare.

options.text: To abolish the worship at the temple.
options.note: The letter supports temple worship generously, not its abolition.

quiz.q: How did Ezra respond to the king's favorable letter?

options.text: He blessed the LORD, who had put it into the king's heart to honor God's house.
options.note: Verses 27-28 record Ezra's doxology crediting God.

options.text: He boasted of his own persuasive skill before the king.
options.note: Ezra credited God, not his own persuasion.

options.text: He kept the credit and the treasure for himself.
options.note: He blessed God and gathered leaders for the mission, not himself.

options.text: He refused the commission as too dangerous.
options.note: He took courage and gathered leaders to return with him.

quiz.q: How does Ezra's ministry of the word point to Christ?

options.text: Ezra studied, obeyed, and taught God's word, foreshadowing Christ, the true teacher who is the Word made flesh.
options.note: Ezra's devotion to Scripture points to Christ, who fulfills and embodies it.

options.text: It shows that God's word is no longer important once Christ comes.
options.note: Christ fulfills and honors the word; He does not make it unimportant.

options.text: It teaches that only priests may read the Bible.
options.note: Ezra taught the Law to all the people, not a privileged few.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ.
options.note: Ezra the teacher-priest points directly to Christ the Teacher and Word.

questions: Ezra set his heart to study God's word, to obey it, and then to teach it. Which of those three is weakest in your own life right now, and what would it take to strengthen it?
Ezra credited every success to the good hand of God upon him. Where are you tempted to credit your own skill or good fortune instead of God's hand?
Ezra pursued Scripture wholeheartedly, not as a hobby but as the shape of his life. What would it look like for God's word to move from the margins to the center of your days?
God moved a pagan king to fund and empower His work. How does that free you from thinking God's purposes depend on favorable circumstances?
Ezra took courage because God's hand was upon him, and gathered others to join the mission. Where do you need courage to step out, trusting that God goes with you?

translationNotes.note: The journey began on the first day of the first month, the season of Passover, subtly framing Ezra's return as a kind of new exodus under the gracious hand of God.
From verse 12 through verse 26 the original text is in Aramaic, the language of the royal letter; the narrative resumes in Hebrew at verse 27 with Ezra's blessing.
The Law of your God renders the Aramaic dat, the ordinary word for a royal decree, showing God's law carried into Judah with imperial backing yet resting on a higher authority.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Ezra 7:1-28. Em dashes in verses 5 and 25 were rendered as commas per house style. Words from 7:12-26 are marked Aramaic and from 7:1-11 and 27-28 Hebrew. No em or en dashes remain.
